analogy: Think of "Cold War Era" through the chapter's own opening images: O verview The Berlin Wall, which had been built at the height of the Cold War and was its greatest symbol, was toppled by the people in 1989. This dramatic event was followed by an equally dramatic and historic chain of events that led to... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: LEADERS OF THE, SOVIET UNION, TIMELINE OF DISINTEGRATION, OF THE SOVIET UNION. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Cold War Era is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that o verview The Berlin Wall, which had been built at the height of the Cold War and was its greatest symbol, was toppled by the people in 1989.

The text explains that this dramatic event was followed by an equally dramatic and historic chain of events that led to the collapse of the ‘second world’ and the end of the Cold War.

The text explains that germany, divided after the Second World War, was unified.

The text explains that one after another, the eight East European countries that were part of the Soviet bloc replaced their communist governments in response to mass demonstrations.

The text explains that the Soviet Union stood by as the Cold War began to end, not by military means but as a result of mass actions by ordinary men and women.

The text explains that eventually the Soviet Union itself disintegrated.

The text explains that in this chapter, we discuss the meaning, the causes and the consequences of the disintegration of the ‘second world’.

The text explains that we also discuss what happened to that part of the world after the collapse of communist regimes and how India relates to these countries now.

The text explains that chapter 1 The End of Bipolarity The Berlin Wall symbolised the division between the capitalist and the communist world.

The text explains that built in 1961 to separate East Berlin from West Berlin, this more than 150 kilometre long wall stood for 28 years and was finally broken by the people on 9 November 1989.

The text explains that this marked the unification of the two parts of Germany and the beginning of the end of the communist bloc.

The text explains that a section of the wall opened to allow free movement 3.

The text explains that the Berlin Wall as it stood before 1989 Credit: 1. and 2.

The text explains that frederik Ramm, www.remote.org/frederik/culture/berlin 3. www.cs.utah.edu

The text explains that contemporary World Politics 2 machinery production, and a transport sector that connected its remotest areas with efficiency.

The text explains that it had a domestic consumer industry that produced everything from pins to cars, though their quality did not match that of the Western capitalist countries.

The text explains that the Soviet state ensured a minimum standard of living for all citizens, and the government subsidised basic necessities including health, education, childcare and other welfare schemes.

The text explains that the Soviet system, however, became very bureaucratic and authoritarian, making life very difficult for its citizens.

The text explains that most of the institutions of the Soviet state needed reform: the one- party system represented by the Communist Party of the Soviet Union had tight control over all institutions and was unaccountable to the people.

The text explains that the revolution was inspired by the ideals of socialism, as opposed to capitalism, and the need for an egalitarian society.

The text explains that this was perhaps the biggest attempt in human history to abolish the institution of private property and consciously design a society based on principles of equality.

The text explains that in doing so, the makers of the Soviet system gave primacy to the state and the institution of the party.

The text explains that the Soviet political system centred around the communist party, and no other political party or opposition was allowed.

The text explains that the economy was planned and controlled by the state.

The text explains that the political and the economic systems of all these countries were modelled after the USSR.

The text explains that the Soviet economy was then more developed than the rest of the world except for the US.

The text explains that it had a complex communications network, vast energy resources including oil, iron and steel, Vladimir Lenin (1870-1924) Founder of the Bolshevik Communist party; leader of the Russian Revolution of 1917 and the founder-head of the USSR during the most difficult period following the revolution (1917-1924); a practitioner of Marxism and a source of inspiration for communists all over the world.

The text explains that the Soviet Union lagged behind the West in technology, infrastructure (e.g., transport, power), and most importantly, in fulfilling the political or economic aspirations of citizens.

The text explains that the Soviet invasion of Afghanistan in 1979 weakened the system even further.

The text explains that the Soviet economy was faltering in the late 1970s and became stagnant.

The text explains that gorbachev and the disinteGration Mikhail Gorbachev, who had become General Secretary of the Communist Party of the Soviet Union in 1985, sought to reform this system.

The text explains that the people in the East European countries which were part of the Soviet bloc started to protest against their own governments and Soviet control.

The text explains that this is a question worth asking not just to understand the Soviet Union and the end of communism but also because it is not the first and may not be the last political system to collapse.

The text explains that while there are unique features of the Soviet collapse, there may be more general lessons to be drawn from this very important case.

The text explains that t h e r e i s n o d o u b t t h a t the inter nal weaknesses of Soviet political and economic institutions, which failed to meet the aspirations of the people, were responsible for the collapse of the system.

The text explains that economic stagnation for many years led to severe consumer shortages and a large section of Soviet society began to doubt and question the system and to do so openly.

The text explains that why did the system become so weak and why did the economy stagnate?

The text explains that t h e S o v i e t e c o n o m y used much of its resources in maintaining a nuclear and military arsenal and the development of its satellite states in Eastern Europe and within the Soviet system (the five Central Asian Republics in particular).

The text explains that this led to a huge economic burden that the system could not cope with.

The text explains that they could see the disparities between their system and the systems of the West.

The text explains that how could so many sensitive people all over the world admire a system like this?

The text explains that the End of Bipolarity 5 system was better than Western capitalism, the reality of its backwardness came as a political and psychological shock.

The text explains that ordinary people were alienated by slow and stifling administration, rampant corruption, the inability of the system to correct mistakes it had made, the unwillingness to allow more openness in government, and the centralisation of authority in a vast land.

The text explains that people did not identify with the system and with the rulers, and the government increasingly lost popular backing.

The text explains that gorbachev promised to reform the economy, catch up with the West, and loosen the administrative system.

The text explains that the most basic answer seems to be that when Gorbachev carried out his reforms and loosened the system, he set in motion forces and expectations that few could have predicted and became virtually impossible to control.

The text explains that there were sections of Soviet society which felt that Gorbachev should have moved much faster and were disappointed and impatient with his methods.

The text explains that others, especially members of the Communist Party and those who were served by the system, took exactly the opposite view.

The text explains that one view is that nationalist urges and feelings were very much at work throughout the history of the Soviet Union and that whether or not the reforms had occurred there would have been an internal struggle within the Soviet Union.

The text explains that end of the ideological war between the US and USSR b.

The text explains that change in the balance of power in the world order d.

The text explains that contemporary World Politics 14 E x e r c i s e s 5.

Source: NCERT Class 12 Contemporary World Politics, Chapter 1: Cold War Era

prelims_traps: Do not treat LEADERS OF THE and SOVIET UNION as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Cold War Era; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Cold War Era through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use LEADERS OF THE and SOVIET UNION to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
